paragraphs: Puget Sound Therapeutics is developing a global-scale corneal endothelial cell therapy that, thanks to cryopreservation, enables each dose to be delivered at under $500 – nearly 100 times less expensive than current treatments – making blindness-curing therapy accessible to millions who currently have no options. The underlying cell therapy has already been clinically proven and was commercially validated through Alcon's $1B+ acquisition of first-generation developer Aurion Biotech. PST's cofounders were on Aurion's founding team and have built the missing piece: a process for freezing, storing, and shipping the therapy worldwide. They're raising $5M at a $25M post-money valuation to complete their Phase I clinical trial, with a targeted strategic exit after the Phase I/II, following the Aurion playbook.

I wrote this memo to walk you through the opportunity the way I'd explain it over coffee. There are materials to go deeper throughout the data room, and as always, if you have questions, give me a call.
Now, let's jump in.

To set the stage, there are roughly 13 million people worldwide who need a corneal transplant due to corneal endothelial disease — conditions like Fuchs' dystrophy, where the cells that keep your cornea clear slowly die and don't come back. For most of them, there's no treatment available.

Right now, the only option is a corneal transplant, which is a complex surgery that relies on donated tissue from someone who has passed away. Globally, donor tissue is scarce: for every 70 people who need a cornea, only one donor is available. So, the current system matches one donor to one patient, but demand is seventy times higher. In contrast, the new therapy can create over 1,000 doses from a single donor, completely changing the equation. As it stands, more than half the world has no access to treatment, and the current approach will never meet the need.

To make matters worse, this disease is genetic. Approximately 4% of people aged 40 or older have some form of endothelial disease and an estimated 16 million people in the US, EU, and Japan alone have some form of endothelial disease, with millions more worldwide who will develop this condition as they age.

There is hope, though. Over the past 15 years, researchers have found that endothelial cells from a single donated cornea can be cultured in the lab to produce over 1,000 doses. This removes the one-donor, one-transplant limitation. Academic clinical trials have confirmed this, and the therapy is already available in Japan.

The first commercial debut came from Aurion Biotech, which brought this therapy to market in Japan and is advancing to its Phase III trial in the US. Alcon acquired Aurion last year for over $1 billion, a landmark validation of both the science and the market demand for a corneal cell therapy.

But here's the catch: Aurion's therapy uses fresh cells, which share the same fundamental constraint as donor tissue — a limited supply. Fresh cells have a short shelf life and require regional manufacturing near the patient, and as a result, the therapy costs around $60,000 per dose. This first-generation therapy proved the concept, but it didn't solve the problem. The millions of people who need this most are still shut out.
The main question now is not if cell therapy can cure corneal blindness, but whether it can be scaled up.

This is the problem Daniel and Ramsey set out to solve when they started Puget Sound Therapeutics. Both were part of Aurion Biotech's founding team, where they saw firsthand the limits of the first generation and realized that cryopreservation was the key to making the therapy available worldwide.

PST's method brings together two proven elements: cell therapy, which has been validated by more than ten years of clinical research, and cryopreservation, a technology already used to store and distribute cell therapies in fields like cancer and transplant medicine. In short, they are developing a second-generation corneal endothelial cell therapy that can be frozen, stored for long periods, and shipped anywhere in the world for less than $500 per dose. Compared to the current $60,000 price, this could dramatically expand who can get treated, where, and on what scale.

Last spring, Daniel and Ramsey raised a pre-seed round to test their main ideas, and they succeeded. Starting with an empty lab in June, they produced over 2 billion cells from 6 different cell lines and made more than 1,500 research-grade cryopreserved doses. They also found unique biomarkers that strengthen their intellectual property. Their process produces high-quality endothelial cells at rates 50 to 100 times higher than previously thought possible, which is a big deal for scaling up. I have spent several days in the lab and seen their progress myself. What they achieved in seven months, under budget and ahead of schedule, is very impressive.
A big reason I believe in this opportunity is the team.

Daniel has worked in this field for more than ten years. After spending a few years at Edwards Life Sciences, he joined the founding team at CorneaGen, which later spun out Aurion Biotech. He led Aurion's clinical trials outside the US, including in El Salvador, and built strong relationships with scientists, doctors, and potential acquirers in ophthalmology. Few people have a deeper network in this area, and it shows in the quality of advisors and investors he has brought together—many have held leadership roles at companies that PST could eventually partner with or be acquired by.

His cofounder, Ramsey Saleem, is PST's Chief Scientist. He has a PhD in Ocular Genetics, was also part of Aurion's founding team, and is an expert in process and assay development. He is the one building and testing the manufacturing process that makes the therapy possible.

Together, they recognized the limits of the first generation, saw the potential of cryopreservation, and founded PST to complete the job. This team has done this before, understands the process, and has the relationships needed to make it happen.

If you look up the market size for corneal endothelial treatment today, you'll find estimates around $500 million per year. But this number is much lower than the real opportunity because the current market is limited by supply. It only shows the treatments that happen, not the true demand.

Even with a conservative estimate, just replacing traditional transplants in developed countries is already a market worth over $1 billion. If you include treating all diagnosed endothelial disease in developed markets and replacing transplants worldwide, the market grows to over $10 billion. When you add in the global population with endothelial disease and the potential to use PST's cryopreservation technology for other cell therapies, the total market exceeds $20 billion.

This is why the opportunity is often underestimated. Many people see the $500 million figure and look for what they think are bigger opportunities. But they miss the huge unmet demand from millions who would get treatment if it were simpler, more affordable, or available. PST's technology is what makes that possible.

The standard approach in biotech is clear: prove a therapy works in clinical trials, then sell to a larger company that can bring it to market worldwide. This is exactly the plan Daniel is following, as he believes a strategic acquisition is the quickest way to get this therapy to patients everywhere.
Here's how it maps out:

Seed (Now): PST is raising $5M at a $25M post-money valuation to fund an outside the US Phase I clinical trial. Going outside the US gets them to initial clinical data faster and more cost-effectively, and Daniel has already run trials in El Salvador.

Series A (around 2027): After getting results from Phase I, PST plans to raise more money at a $75 to $125 million valuation to fund a combined Phase I/II trial. This would be a 3-5x increase from the seed round and is the only planned round before an expected exit.

Strategic Exit (around 2029): After the Phase I/II results, PST will look for a strategic acquisition. This is the same stage when Alcon bought Aurion for over $1 billion, and the team is already talking with potential buyers.
Timelines in biotech can change, of course. But given how well the team has performed so far, I believe they will meet and potentially exceed these timelines.
Beyond the fundamentals, a few trends make this an especially good time to invest.

First, major pharma and medtech companies together have over $2 trillion in M&A firepower as their top-earning patents expire. Companies like Johnson & Johnson, Regeneron, and Eli Lilly are all actively acquiring in ophthalmology, and Lilly just made its first eye care acquisitions in 2025. Meanwhile, early-stage biotech funding has been limited in recent years, so fewer strong acquisition targets are available. Companies like PST that raised capital and made real progress during that period are squarely in the crosshairs of that demand.

Second, the FDA just introduced more flexible manufacturing requirements for cell therapies, reducing the need for comparability studies and process qualification. As a result, potential buyers now have a more cost-effective and streamlined path to market compared to the previous year.

Finally, the Aurion acquisition is proof of market demand and the value of corneal cell therapy, as well as the limits of the first-generation approach. The companies that tried to buy Aurion but missed out are now searching for the next big opportunity, and a second-generation therapy with better economics and global reach fits that need.
Naturally, it's important to talk about risks. There are real uncertainties, and you should have the full picture.

The biggest question is whether cryopreserved cells will work as well in people as they have in the lab. The Phase I trial will answer this. The good news is that Aurion has already proven the core cell therapy in clinical trials. PST is not creating a new therapy, but improving how it is made and delivered. Still, we do not yet have human data, so this is a real risk.

It's important to know that the outcome is not all-or-nothing. Results can fall anywhere along a range. If cryopreserved cells work as well as or close to fresh cells in humans, PST will be in a strong position for acquisition. If the results are a bit lower but still meaningful, the team may need to adjust the formula, which could take more time and money. If the approach does not work in humans at all, that would be a bigger setback, but the team's unique biomarker discoveries and manufacturing know-how could still have value.
There are other risks to consider: regulations could change, competitors could emerge, and PST will need to raise a Series A round to move past Phase I.

To sum up, this therapy's science has been proven in clinical trials and through a billion-dollar acquisition. The first generation showed it works, but could not scale. The same team started PST to build the next version, and they have already shown their approach works technically. Market conditions are very favorable, and the plan follows a proven path in this field.

The main risk is moving from lab results to success in people. But the core therapy is proven, the team has experience running these trials, and the money being raised will fund this key step. With a $25 million entry point and a realistic chance for a 10-20x return in 3-4 years, this is a risk-reward profile that's hard to ignore.
I believe this is a bet worth making, both for the potential returns and for the impact it will have.
